sections.h:
COMMISSIONER’S MESSAGE

I am proud to present to you the revised WA Police Force Investigation Doctrine (the Doctrine). The original version was created to address criticism regarding the manner in which we conduct investigations. Since then we have further learnt and evolved; and whilst we are at the ten-year mark, we still face the challenge of ensuring our Force’s practices are flexible and responsive to a changing policing and investigative environment.

The original version of the Doctrine fostered effective communication and collaboration between generalist and specialist police by providing a common investigative language. The common usage of the terms The5KIS and the CRIME model are evidence of its success.

We now find ourselves in an ever evolving environment with competing demands on police resources and a need to find efficiency gains and better ways of doing our business. This version of the Doctrine builds on the successes of the original and balances the key fundamentals of effectiveness and efficiency, without relaxing our key goal of improving the quality of investigations whilst maintaining and building on our standards of practice.

There is a duty for supervisors to make quality decisions based on prioritisation, categorisation and allocation to ensure matters are investigated in a timely and efficient manner.

Investigation permeates every aspect of policing within Western Australia. It is the responsibility of all members of the WA Police Force to know, understand and apply the principles of the Doctrine without fear, favour or prejudice to ensure all investigations are conducted appropriately and in an honest and accountable manner.

The need to embrace the Doctrine is binding on all WA Police officers, consistent with our mission to provide trusted and valued policing for Western Australia.

CHRIS DAWSON APM

COMMISSIONER OF POLICE

sections.t: Introduction — purpose, scope and management of the Doctrine
sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

Purpose of Doctrine

Doctrine is the codification of beliefs, instructions or taught principles in a given branch of knowledge or a belief system. Doctrine provides philosophical guidance on the way an organisation conducts business.

Purpose of the WA Police Force Investigation Doctrine

The Doctrine is the cornerstone of the Western Australia Police Force’s (WA Police Force) commitment to quality investigations.

The Doctrine is based on proven investigative and resource management principles. It is firm in its intent but requires judgement in its application.

The Doctrine applies to all situations where a systematic approach and careful examination is required. It is equally applicable to criminal and non-criminal investigations. It promotes and standardises good investigative practice by providing a framework of investigation ownership and accountability.

The Doctrine outlines the CRIME Model and The Five Key Investigative Strategies (The5KIS) both of which guide investigators in making logical, structured and accountable decisions.

Scope of the Document

The Doctrine applies to all WA Police Force investigators involved in the conduct of investigations. It provides a narrative on:

• The investigative environment

• The attributes which promote good investigative practice

• The key principles of a good investigation

• Overall investigation management.

The WA Police Force publishes numerous documents to support the Doctrine; however, the Doctrine underpins all investigative practices for the WA Police Force.

This is a public document and may become disclosable in judicial proceedings.

Doctrine Management

The intent of the Doctrine should not be hampered due to organisational or terminology changes, any reference to a specific term includes the current equivalent. The Doctrine is a corporately endorsed document and any significant amendments or modifications must have the approval of the Commissioner of Police or his delegate.

Any minor amendments or modifications must have the approval of the Divisional Detective Inspector, governing the agency’s Investigative Standards Unit.

sections.t: Investigation Principles — environment, legal framework, values, attributes, critical thinking, decisions, iLevels
sections.h:
INVESTIGATION PRINCIPLES

The Investigative Environment

The WA Police Force is the public face of law enforcement in Western Australia. Quality investigations contribute significantly to the community’s expectations and our mission:

To provide trusted and valued policing for Western Australia.

It is the responsibility of the WA Police Force to investigate matters in an impartial manner.

Investigators will not have a vested interest in one party over another; and will always maintain a neutral position, remaining objective at all times. The WA Police Force encourages a collegiate approach to investigations.

Investigators will be called upon to conduct investigations into a variety of crime types each with individual complexity. Investigators must be astute to the dynamic nature of each investigation and apply judgement and innovation with a goal of successful resolution.

Successful resolution includes the completion of an investigation which has been undertaken thoroughly, efficiently and ethically. Success should not solely be measured on arrest rates or charges preferred.

The investigative environment does not cease at resolution. Investigative information is held in perpetuity and may be the subject of internal and external reviews. In these instances, the investigative standards may be held to account years later.

Adherence to the Legal Framework

WA Police Force investigations must be conducted in accordance with statute law, common law and established legal principles. Investigators must be conversant with relevant law to ensure the conduct of the investigation is legally defensible and evidentially sound.

Adherence to Values

Underpinning all WA policing are our values of Duty, Teamwork, Integrity and Care.

To further enhance our values there are two additional principles specific to investigations, commitment and transparency. In abiding by these principles, investigators ensure investigations are completed with the highest integrity and in accordance with community expectations.

Investigative Attributes

Good investigators combine the attributes of tenacity and objectivity within well-established legal and ethical parameters to conduct a thorough, professional and impartial search for the truth. They apply critical thinking and innovation in the development of investigative strategies which are adaptable and scalable in application. They are always current with relevant laws, governance and investigative techniques.

A good investigator is logical, intuitive and accountable. Their decisions are reasonable, proportionate and necessary.

Critical Thinking

Critical thinking is an investigator’s ability to look inward and understand how and why a particular decision was made. It requires an ability to identify personal experiences and unintended biases which may impact on the decision making process.

Good investigators must be able to take their thinking apart systematically, to analyse each part, assess it for quality and improve it. This will result in an investigation that is innovative, balanced and focussed towards a successful outcome.

Recording Decisions

By applying critical thinking, investigators will ultimately make balanced and justified decisions. All decisions associated with an investigation must be recorded on a recognised WA Police Force system.

Investigator Levels (iLevels)

Crucial to the successful resolution of an investigation is the deployment of suitably trained and experienced officers. The WA Police Force uses the iLevels to ensure the management of an investigation is conducted by an appropriately qualified individual.

sections.t: Investigation Ownership — jurisdiction, multi-agency, allocation of responsibility
sections.h:
INVESTIGATION OWNERSHIP

Offences Outside of Western Australia

Where an offence has occurred outside of Western Australia, ownership of the investigation rests with the jurisdiction where the offence occurred. When the WA Police Force is requested to assist or required to respond, the response should be the same as if the offence occurred within Western Australia.

Multi-Agency or Multi-Jurisdictional Investigations

If the WA Police Force engages in a multi-agency or multi-jurisdictional investigation the investigative standards are the responsibility of the lead agency. Where the WA Police Force is the lead agency, the Doctrine will apply.

Offences within Western Australia

Responsibility for investigations typically rests with the district superintendent where the offence occurred. Agency standards or circumstances may require allocation of the investigation to another district or division. Where an investigation is reallocated, the relevant superintendent assumes responsibility. Reallocation of serious offences may require a formal handover.

The WA Police Force will ensure timely and professional investigations are conducted, by clearly determining and communicating ownership. The business unit assigned the investigation retains ownership of the offence and must appoint an Investigating Officer (IO).

Where the WA Police Force is the lead agency, the Doctrine will apply. Agency standards or circumstances may require allocation of the investigation to another district or division. Where an investigation is reallocated, the relevant superintendent assumes responsibility. Reallocation of serious offences may require a formal handover. The business unit assigned the investigation retains ownership of the offence and must appoint an Investigating Officer (IO).

sections.t: The 'CRIME' Model — overview of the five elements
sections.h:
THE ‘CRIME’ MODEL

Good practice is achieved by distilling a process into its critical elements and designing a framework based on expertise and experience. The investigative framework used by the WA Police Force is the CRIME model which assists

practitioners, supervisors and the agency conduct quality investigations.

The CRIME model reflects the steps of a typical investigation by highlighting the five critical elements namely Contact, Respond, Investigate, Manage and Evaluate.
sections.b: THE ‘CRIME’ MODEL Good practice is achieved by distilling a process into its critical elements and designing a framework based on expertise and experience. The investigative framework used by the WA Police Force is the CRIME model which assists practitioners, supervisors and the agency conduct quality investigations. The CRIME model reflects the steps of a typical investigation by highlighting the five critical elements namely Contact, Respond, Investigate, Manage and Evaluate.

sections.h:
Contact

Contact is the initial step of an investigation. It is the first opportunity to engage with the reporting person and instil confidence and trust in the WA Police Force. The Initial Contact Officer (ICO) whether sworn or police staff, must demonstrate good communication and empathy to maximise rapport and disclosure.

Crucial to the Contact step is the ICO’s ability to swiftly and accurately elicit information, to ascertain the exact nature of the allegation, identify appropriate police response and maximise the early collection of evidential material.

All of the ICO’s skills share equal importance in setting the professional tone for the subsequent investigation.
sections.b: Contact Contact is the initial step of an investigation. It is the first opportunity to engage with the reporting person and instil confidence and trust in the WA Police Force. The Initial Contact Officer (ICO) whether sworn or police staff, must demonstrate good communication and empathy to maximise rapport and disclosure. Crucial to the Contact step is the ICO’s ability to swiftly and accurately elicit information, to ascertain the exact nature of the allegation, identify appropriate police response and maximise the early collection of evidential material. All of the ICO’s skills share equal importance in setting the professional tone for the subsequent investigation.

sections.h:
Respond

Respond in the CRIME model represents the reaction to the initial contact. It has a wide application ranging from telephone response to scene attendance. Police will manage response in accordance with policy, agency standards and statutory requirements. The protection of life and property will always override evidentiary collection.

The Respond step of the investigation aims to determine what offence has been committed and whether further investigation is required or if it may be finalised at that point.

The role of responding officers is not simply to attend an incident. They must be inquisitive, organised and apply an investigative mindset to identify potential avenues of inquiry.
sections.b: Respond Respond in the CRIME model represents the reaction to the initial contact. It has a wide application ranging from telephone response to scene attendance. Police will manage response in accordance with policy, agency standards and statutory requirements. The protection of life and property will always override evidentiary collection. The Respond step of the investigation aims to determine what offence has been committed and whether further investigation is required or if it may be finalised at that point. The role of responding officers is not simply to attend an incident. They must be inquisitive, organised and apply an investigative mindset to identify potential avenues of inquiry.

sections.h:
Investigate

‘Investigate’ is typically the major component of the CRIME model but should not be considered more important than the other stages. It

promotes an objective search for evidentiary material in order to establish the truth.

Police investigations must be organised, systematic and scaled to the offence. To encourage and prompt a thorough search for evidence, the WA Police Force has The5KIS.

The5KIS create a common investigative language which

standardises planning, briefings, and the management of investigations. Each of the key investigative strategies represents an area or discipline in which evidence may be located namely; Witnesses, Suspects, Physical Material, Intelligence and Public Awareness.

The5KIS maximise investigative opportunities and prompt investigators to look broadly when identifying lines of inquiry. The strategies must all be considered, but not all will yield lines of inquiry at a given time. The5KIS need to be continually re-assessed in an attempt to fill information gaps. They can be applied in any order, concurrently or in isolation.
sections.b: Investigate ‘Investigate’ is typically the major component of the CRIME model but should not be considered more important than the other stages. It promotes an objective search for evidentiary material in order to establish the truth. Police investigations must be organised, systematic and scaled to the offence. To encourage and prompt a thorough search for evidence, the WA Police Force has The5KIS. The5KIS create a common investigative language which standardises planning, briefings, and the management of investigations. Each of the key investigative strategies represents an area or discipline in which evidence may be located namely; Witnesses, Suspects, Physical Material, Intelligence and Public Awareness. The5KIS maximise investigative opportunities and prompt investigators to look broadly when identifying lines of inquiry. The strategies must all be considered, but not all will yield lines of inquiry at a given time. The5KIS need to be continually re-assessed in an attempt to fill information gaps. They can be applied in any order, concurrently or in isolation.

sections.h:
Witnesses

This strategy is used to identify, locate and interview witnesses to an incident in order to elicit information. Witnesses are categorised dependant on their knowledge of the offence and interviewed in accordance with fairness, legislation and WA Police Force policies and procedures.

The WA Police Force categorises witnesses as victim, eye witness, other significant witness, police witness, expert witness and general witness. There is always potential for a witness to become a suspect, at which point they must be afforded their rights.

Witnesses will be interviewed in accordance with the Investigative Interviewing model.

Investigators must be mindful of potential vulnerabilities which may induce suggestibility or result in a flawed account from the witness.

When required the witness strategy should encompass security and wellbeing.
sections.b: Witnesses This strategy is used to identify, locate and interview witnesses to an incident in order to elicit information. Witnesses are categorised dependant on their knowledge of the offence and interviewed in accordance with fairness, legislation and WA Police Force policies and procedures. The WA Police Force categorises witnesses as victim, eye witness, other significant witness, police witness, expert witness and general witness. There is always potential for a witness to become a suspect, at which point they must be afforded their rights. Witnesses will be interviewed in accordance with the Investigative Interviewing model. Investigators must be mindful of potential vulnerabilities which may induce suggestibility or result in a flawed account from the witness. When required the witness strategy should encompass security and wellbeing.

sections.h:
Suspects

The suspect strategy involves the development of lines of inquiry to identify, locate, and interview suspects.

Suspect categorisation assists investigators to prioritise investigative focus. The WA Police Force aligns categorisation to the legal framework. Suspects are categorised in the following way:

• Suspect: A person suspected of having committed an offence, albeit the threshold of

reasonable suspicion has not been reached • Arrestable Suspect: A person reasonably suspected of committing an offence within the meaning of s4 Criminal Investigation Act, 2006 (CIA). This person is arrestable but has not been arrested

• Arrested Suspect: A person arrested pursuant to s128 CIA.

Categorisation is based on the circumstances at the time and is equally applicable to single and multiple suspects. If the circumstances change the category of a suspect may change.

Suspects will be interviewed using the Investigative Interviewing, Conversation Management model. The principles of fairness and voluntariness must always be applied.

Investigators must be mindful of potential vulnerabilities which may induce suggestibility or result in a flawed account from the suspect. (Refer to Investigative Interviewing on the Investigative Standards intranet page at Investigative Standards.)
sections.b: Suspects The suspect strategy involves the development of lines of inquiry to identify, locate, and interview suspects. Suspect categorisation assists investigators to prioritise investigative focus. The WA Police Force aligns categorisation to the legal framework. Suspects are categorised in the following way: • Suspect: A person suspected of having committed an offence, albeit the threshold of reasonable suspicion has not been reached • Arrestable Suspect: A person reasonably suspected of committing an offence within the meaning of s4 Criminal Investigation Act, 2006 (CIA). This person is arrestable but has not been arrested • Arrested Suspect: A person arrested pursuant to s128 CIA. Categorisation is based on the circumstances at the time and is equally applicable to single and multiple suspects. If the circumstances change the category of a suspect may change. Suspects will be interviewed using the Investigative Interviewing, Conversation Management model. The principles of fairness and voluntariness must always be applied. Investigators must be mindful of potential vulnerabilities which may induce suggestibility or result in a flawed account from the suspect. (Refer to Investigative Interviewing on the Investigative Standards intranet page at Investigative Standards.)

sections.h:
Investigative Interviewing

Witnesses and suspects can provide valuable information which assists in establishing the truth of the matter under investigation. Fundamental to the ‘Witness’ and ‘Suspect’ strategies is interviewing.

Well planned interviews can significantly progress an investigation. Planning includes the early identification of the interviewee’s legal status to ensure they are well informed about their rights.

The WA Police Force uses the Investigative Interviewing Model (IIM) when conducting interviews.
sections.b: Investigative Interviewing Witnesses and suspects can provide valuable information which assists in establishing the truth of the matter under investigation. Fundamental to the ‘Witness’ and ‘Suspect’ strategies is interviewing. Well planned interviews can significantly progress an investigation. Planning includes the early identification of the interviewee’s legal status to ensure they are well informed about their rights. The WA Police Force uses the Investigative Interviewing Model (IIM) when conducting interviews.

sections.h:
Physical Material

The Physical Material strategy relates to the development of lines of inquiry to identify items potentially related to the offence. It encompasses all real and documentary evidence.

Physical Material strategies can be considered from two perspectives:

• The identification and collection of physical material

• The analysis, treatment or management of the physical material to link it to an incident

or person.
sections.b: Physical Material The Physical Material strategy relates to the development of lines of inquiry to identify items potentially related to the offence. It encompasses all real and documentary evidence. Physical Material strategies can be considered from two perspectives: • The identification and collection of physical material • The analysis, treatment or management of the physical material to link it to an incident or person.

sections.h:
Intelligence

The Intelligence strategy aims to identify information or sources that add value to investigations. In context, intelligence may be based on information, supposition or reasoning supported by facts. Intelligence can help link multiple offences or link an offender to an offence.
sections.b: Intelligence The Intelligence strategy aims to identify information or sources that add value to investigations. In context, intelligence may be based on information, supposition or reasoning supported by facts. Intelligence can help link multiple offences or link an offender to an offence.

sections.h:
Public Awareness

The Public Awareness strategy is used to promote public support and reassurance, elicit information and ensure public safety.

Great care must be taken to ensure the Public Awareness strategy does not compromise the investigation; prosecution; contaminate potential witness and suspect accounts;

jeopardise community confidence or safety; or expose the WA Police Force or the State to civil liability. Caution should be applied when discussing the status of a particular person. Unless it forms a specific strategy, police should avoid any term that could be construed by the community as a descriptor for offender.
sections.b: Public Awareness The Public Awareness strategy is used to promote public support and reassurance, elicit information and ensure public safety. Great care must be taken to ensure the Public Awareness strategy does not compromise the investigation; prosecution; contaminate potential witness and suspect accounts; jeopardise community confidence or safety; or expose the WA Police Force or the State to civil liability. Caution should be applied when discussing the status of a particular person. Unless it forms a specific strategy, police should avoid any term that could be construed by the community as a descriptor for offender.

sections.h:
Manage

‘Manage’ within the CRIME Model has its own specific requirements, it applies to all stages within the model and ensures overall good governance of the investigation.

Manage is applied at three distinct levels:

• Agency: Responsible for establishing investigative practices

• Supervisor: Responsible for compliance with investigative practice

• Individual: Responsible for the application of investigative practice.

Agency

The WA Police Force will provide infrastructure and resources to meet investigative demands. It is responsible for establishing good practice and communicating the need for standardised professional investigations.

The agency will implement and maintain a management structure which quality assures investigations and identifies training relevant to the needs of the investigator, community expectations and disclosure.

The agency evaluates investigative performance to ensure continuous improvement.

Supervision

District or Divisional Officers will actively promote and ensure compliance with the Doctrine.

Officers in Charge (OIC) and supervisors will implement procedures and practice to ensure compliance with the Doctrine.

The OIC and supervisors commit to:

• maintaining investigative skills and knowledge commensurate with their role

• mentoring staff by identifying training needs

• reviewing investigations for compliance with the Doctrine.

Individual

The Investigating Officer (IO) and/or Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) will:

• conduct investigations in adherence with the Doctrine

• identify and prioritise potential investigative actions by using The5KIS

• apply investigative practices in accordance with relevant legislation

• comply with agency policy and guidelines

• commit to their own professional development

• consider disclosure obligations from the commencement of the investigation.
sections.b: Manage ‘Manage’ within the CRIME Model has its own specific requirements, it applies to all stages within the model and ensures overall good governance of the investigation. Manage is applied at three distinct levels: • Agency: Responsible for establishing investigative practices • Supervisor: Responsible for compliance with investigative practice • Individual: Responsible for the application of investigative practice. Agency The WA Police Force will provide infrastructure and resources to meet investigative demands. It is responsible for establishing good practice and communicating the need for standardised professional investigations. The agency will implement and maintain a management structure which quality assures investigations and identifies training relevant to the needs of the investigator, community expectations and disclosure. The agency evaluates investigative performance to ensure continuous improvement. Supervision District or Divisional Officers will actively promote and ensure compliance with the Doctrine. Officers in Charge (OIC) and supervisors will implement procedures and practice to ensure compliance with the Doctrine. The OIC and supervisors commit to: • maintaining investigative skills and knowledge commensurate with their role • mentoring staff by identifying training needs • reviewing investigations for compliance with the Doctrine. Individual The Investigating Officer (IO) and/or Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) will: • conduct investigations in adherence with the Doctrine • identify and prioritise potential investigative actions by using The5KIS • apply investigative practices in accordance with relevant legislation • comply with agency policy and guidelines • commit to their own professional development • consider disclosure obligations from the commencement of the investigation.

sections.t: Dimensions of an Investigation — prioritisation, categorisation, allocation, IO/SIO appointment
sections.h:
Dimensions of an Investigation

Every investigation can be characterised in terms of priority, category and allocation.

Prioritisation

Prioritisation applies to all aspects of the investigation. Investigation prioritisation is based on:

• risk to victim or community safety

• potential for the loss of evidence

• potential for significant undermining of agency reputation and public confidence.

Categorisation

The WA Police Force has three investigation categories; confined, compound and complex.

Investigations are categorised to ensure allocation to appropriately resourced business units and to assist supervisors assess the investigation’s impact on a business unit’s capacity.

Categorisation is not static and as an investigation evolves, it may be necessary to recategorise it. Categorisation is the responsibility of the OIC or Supervisor.

In the event an investigation category needs to be arbitrated, it will be referred to the Assistant District or Divisional Officer or the District/Divisional Officer.

Confined investigations involve a limited number of investigative actions and minimal human resources.

Compound investigations involve a significant number of investigative actions but limited human resources; or a limited number of investigative actions but significant human resources.

Complex investigations involve a significant number of investigative actions and a significant number of human resources.

Allocation Generally

The allocation of an investigation is not to be delayed for administrative purposes. Allocation of investigations is based on the following criteria:

• the category of the investigation

• the business unit resource capacity

• the required skill and knowledge of the investigator or investigation team.

Investigation allocation will be based on investigator iLevels.

Uniformed Police (i1 to i3)

Generally, uniformed police should be allocated simple and ‘either way’ offences that are confined or compound. In all instances officers must have the contemporary skills to conduct the investigation and have access to adequate guidance. This does not preclude uniformed officers from undertaking investigative actions for any investigation. District Detective Office (i4 to i7) District detectives are generally allocated complex ‘either way’ offences and purely indictable offences. The district must have the resources to undertake the investigation. The IO must have contemporary skills and knowledge to conduct the investigation or have access to adequate guidance.

Specialist Investigation Units/Squads (i4 to i7)

Specialist investigation units will be allocated offences which fall within their charter. In general, this includes purely indictable offences and ‘either way’ offences which are compound or complex. The SIO/IO must have the contemporary skills and knowledge to conduct the investigation.

Allocation to an Investigating Officer

The Doctrine does not limit investigators of any iLevel being the initial responder to an incident, neither does it restrict any investigator from completing subsequent investigative actions. Rather, the focus is on the ongoing management of an investigation to ensure it is allocated to suitable business units and managed by appropriately qualified investigators.

An IO will be appointed in all instances. Consideration must be given to their accredited investigation level (iLevel), skills, knowledge, experience and availability.

Indictable offences that cannot be heard summarily will be investigated by a current serving detective (minimum i4). Where this is not possible, another officer with sufficient investigative skills may be appointed the IO under the management of a substantive Detective Sergeant.

Responsibility for an investigation is always retained by the business unit. Where an IO is transferred or absent for a significant period of time, the investigation will be re-allocated to another IO to ensure victim support and investigative continuity. Where an IO has intimate knowledge of an investigation, responsibility may be transferred with the IO to another business unit.

The Investigating Officer

The IO is responsible for conducting the investigation in compliance with the Doctrine and legal framework. The IO will identify and communicate any issues which may impact the quality of the investigation to their supervisor.

In the majority of cases, an IO will be responsible for management of all investigative actions.

However, in some instances the complexity of the investigation may require additional specialist investigation roles.

Senior Investigating Officer (SIO)

Divisional or district superintendents may appoint a SIO when an investigation requires senior leadership. The SIO will be a Detective Senior Sergeant or above, i7 qualified and have the requisite skills and experience in the specific crime type.

The SIO should be given relief from their normal duties to meet the demands of the investigation.
sections.b: Dimensions of an Investigation Every investigation can be characterised in terms of priority, category and allocation. Prioritisation Prioritisation applies to all aspects of the investigation. Investigation prioritisation is based on: • risk to victim or community safety • potential for the loss of evidence • potential for significant undermining of agency reputation and public confidence. Categorisation The WA Police Force has three investigation categories; confined, compound and complex. Investigations are categorised to ensure allocation to appropriately resourced business units and to assist supervisors assess the investigation’s impact on a business unit’s capacity. Categorisation is not static and as an investigation evolves, it may be necessary to recategorise it. Categorisation is the responsibility of the OIC or Supervisor. In the event an investigation category needs to be arbitrated, it will be referred to the Assistant District or Divisional Officer or the District/Divisional Officer. Confined investigations involve a limited number of investigative actions and minimal human resources. Compound investigations involve a significant number of investigative actions but limited human resources; or a limited number of investigative actions but significant human resources. Complex investigations involve a significant number of investigative actions and a significant number of human resources. Allocation Generally The allocation of an investigation is not to be delayed for administrative purposes. Allocation of investigations is based on the following criteria: • the category of the investigation • the business unit resource capacity • the required skill and knowledge of the investigator or investigation team. Investigation allocation will be based on investigator iLevels. Uniformed Police (i1 to i3) Generally, uniformed police should be allocated simple and ‘either way’ offences that are confined or compound. In all instances officers must have the contemporary skills to conduct the investigation and have access to adequate guidance. This does not preclude uniformed officers from undertaking investigative actions for any investigation. District Detective Office (i4 to i7) District detectives are generally allocated complex ‘either way’ offences and purely indictable offences. The district must have the resources to undertake the investigation. The IO must have contemporary skills and knowledge to conduct the investigation or have access to adequate guidance. Specialist Investigation Units/Squads (i4 to i7) Specialist investigation units will be allocated offences which fall within their charter. In general, this includes purely indictable offences and ‘either way’ offences which are compound or complex. The SIO/IO must have the contemporary skills and knowledge to conduct the investigation. Allocation to an Investigating Officer The Doctrine does not limit investigators of any iLevel being the initial responder to an incident, neither does it restrict any investigator from completing subsequent investigative actions. Rather, the focus is on the ongoing management of an investigation to ensure it is allocated to suitable business units and managed by appropriately qualified investigators. An IO will be appointed in all instances. Consideration must be given to their accredited investigation level (iLevel), skills, knowledge, experience and availability. Indictable offences that cannot be heard summarily will be investigated by a current serving detective (minimum i4). Where this is not possible, another officer with sufficient investigative skills may be appointed the IO under the management of a substantive Detective Sergeant. Responsibility for an investigation is always retained by the business unit. Where an IO is transferred or absent for a significant period of time, the investigation will be re-allocated to another IO to ensure victim support and investigative continuity. Where an IO has intimate knowledge of an investigation, responsibility may be transferred with the IO to another business unit. The Investigating Officer The IO is responsible for conducting the investigation in compliance with the Doctrine and legal framework. The IO will identify and communicate any issues which may impact the quality of the investigation to their supervisor. In the majority of cases, an IO will be responsible for management of all investigative actions. However, in some instances the complexity of the investigation may require additional specialist investigation roles. Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) Divisional or district superintendents may appoint a SIO when an investigation requires senior leadership. The SIO will be a Detective Senior Sergeant or above, i7 qualified and have the requisite skills and experience in the specific crime type. The SIO should be given relief from their normal duties to meet the demands of the investigation.

sections.h:
CRIME File Management

The WA Police Force has a legislative requirement to retain documents associated with investigations. It is the ethos of WA Police Force to adopt a centralist approach to retention.

CRIME File

The WA Police Force utilises the CRIME file as the individual investigation collation source for documents or items which cannot be retained in a recognised WA Police Force system.

CRIME File Repository

Maintaining a central location for investigative documentation is essential to the efficient and effective management of investigative records, ensuring ready access and accountability.

To supplement agile retrieval of both hardcopy and electronic documents a logical and intuitive approach should be adopted.

CRIME File Repository Maintaining a central location for investigative documentation is essential to the efficient and effective management of investigative records, ensuring ready access and accountability. To supplement agile retrieval of both hardcopy and electronic documents a logical and intuitive approach should be adopted.

sections.t: CRIME — Evaluate (incl. Endorsement to Charge & Investigation Review)
sections.h:
Evaluate

Evaluate is the final stage of the CRIME model and completes the investigative cycle by seeking to identify best practice and measuring current performance against that standard.

The WA Police Force aims to achieve investigative validation through evaluation at three levels:

• Agency

• Supervisor

• Individual.

The Endorsement to Charge (ETC) Process

Within the context of an investigation, the WA Police Force uses the ETC process to comply with the legal framework. The ETC acts as a validation process to ensure prima facie evidence exists prior to a charge being preferred.

Investigation Review

The WA Police Force is committed to conducting investigative reviews using a formal process with clear ‘Terms of Reference’. Investigative reviews will be conducted utilising the CRIME model and ‘The5KIS’ standards.

sections.h:
COMMITMENT

The role of an investigator is a crucial part of policing within Western Australia. All members of the WA Police Force have a professional responsibility to know, understand and apply the principles of the Doctrine.

The need to embrace the Doctrine is binding on all WA Police Force personnel as we move forward to provide trusted and valued policing for Western Australia.
